title: List Active Users
prompt:
The `users` table tracks signups.

```
id | name    | is_active
1  | Alice   | true
2  | Bob     | false
3  | Carol   | true
```

Return `id` and `name` of every active user, ordered by `id`.
schema:
CREATE TABLE users(id INT, name TEXT, is_active BOOLEAN);
INSERT INTO users VALUES(1,'Alice',1),(2,'Bob',0),(3,'Carol',1),(4,'Dan',1);
solution: SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE is_active = 1 ORDER BY id;
hints: SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE is_active = 1 ORDER BY id;

title: Products In Price Range
prompt: Return `name` of products priced between 10 and 50 inclusive, ordered alphabetically.
schema:
CREATE TABLE products(id INT, name TEXT, price REAL);
INSERT INTO products VALUES(1,'Pen',5),(2,'Book',12),(3,'Lamp',45),(4,'Desk',120),(5,'Mug',10);
solution: SELECT name FROM products WHERE price BETWEEN 10 AND 50 ORDER BY name;

title: Names Starting With A
prompt: Return `name` of employees whose name starts with 'A', sorted A→Z.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees(id INT, name TEXT);
INSERT INTO employees VALUES(1,'Alice'),(2,'Adam'),(3,'Bob'),(4,'Anna'),(5,'Carl');
solution: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE name LIKE 'A%' ORDER BY name;

title: Users Without Email
prompt: Return `id` and `name` of users with a NULL email.
schema:
CREATE TABLE users(id INT, name TEXT, email TEXT);
INSERT INTO users VALUES(1,'A','a@x.com'),(2,'B',NULL),(3,'C',NULL),(4,'D','d@x.com');
starter: SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE ___;
solution: SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE email IS NULL ORDER BY id;

title: Users In Selected Cities
prompt: Return `id, name` of users in 'Paris' or 'Lyon', ordered by id.
schema:
CREATE TABLE users(id INT, name TEXT, city TEXT);
INSERT INTO users VALUES(1,'A','Paris'),(2,'B','Berlin'),(3,'C','Lyon'),(4,'D','Paris');
starter: SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE ___;
solution: SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE city IN ('Paris','Lyon') ORDER BY id;

title: Non-Cancelled Orders
prompt: Return `order_id` of orders whose status is anything except 'cancelled', ordered by id.
schema:
CREATE TABLE orders(order_id INT, status TEXT);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES(1,'paid'),(2,'cancelled'),(3,'pending'),(4,'paid');
solution: SELECT order_id FROM orders WHERE status <> 'cancelled' ORDER BY order_id;

title: Top 3 Most Expensive
prompt: Return `name, price` of the 3 most expensive products, highest first.
schema:
CREATE TABLE products(id INT, name TEXT, price REAL);
INSERT INTO products VALUES(1,'A',5),(2,'B',12),(3,'C',45),(4,'D',120),(5,'E',80);
starter: SELECT name, price FROM products ORDER BY ___ LIMIT ___;
solution: SELECT name, price FROM products ORDER BY price DESC LIMIT 3;

title: Distinct Countries
prompt: Return each distinct `country` in alphabetical order.
schema:
CREATE TABLE customers(id INT, country TEXT);
INSERT INTO customers VALUES(1,'US'),(2,'DE'),(3,'US'),(4,'FR'),(5,'DE');
starter: SELECT DISTINCT ___ FROM customers ORDER BY ___;
solution: SELECT DISTINCT country FROM customers ORDER BY country;
hints: SELECT DISTINCT country ... ORDER BY country

title: Sort By Two Columns
prompt: Return all rows ordered by `department` asc, then `salary` desc.
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, department TEXT, salary INT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,'Eng',100),(2,'Sales',90),(3,'Eng',120),(4,'Sales',80);
starter: SELECT id, department, salary FROM emp ORDER BY ___;
solution: SELECT id, department, salary FROM emp ORDER BY department ASC, salary DESC;

title: Second Highest Salary
prompt: Return the second-highest *distinct* salary as `second_highest`. If none, return NULL.
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, salary INT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,100),(2,200),(3,200),(4,150);
solution: SELECT MAX(salary) AS second_highest FROM emp WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM emp);
hints: Find MAX where salary < overall MAX.
SELECT MAX(salary) FROM emp WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM emp)

title: Count Orders
prompt: Return total number of orders as `total`.
schema:
CREATE TABLE orders(id INT);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES(1),(2),(3),(4),(5);
starter: SELECT COUNT(*) AS total FROM orders;
solution: SELECT COUNT(*) AS total FROM orders;
hints: SELECT COUNT(*) AS total FROM orders

title: Average Price
prompt: Return average price as `avg_price`, rounded to 2 decimals.
schema:
CREATE TABLE products(id INT, price REAL);
INSERT INTO products VALUES(1,10),(2,20),(3,30);
starter: SELECT ROUND(AVG(___),2) AS avg_price FROM products;
solution: SELECT ROUND(AVG(price),2) AS avg_price FROM products;

title: Min And Max Price
prompt: Return `min_price` and `max_price` from products.
schema:
CREATE TABLE products(id INT, price REAL);
INSERT INTO products VALUES(1,5),(2,50),(3,15);
starter: SELECT MIN(___) AS min_price, MAX(___) AS max_price FROM products;
solution: SELECT MIN(price) AS min_price, MAX(price) AS max_price FROM products;

title: Unique Customers
prompt: From `orders`, count distinct `customer_id` as `unique_customers`.
schema:
CREATE TABLE orders(id INT, customer_id INT);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES(1,10),(2,11),(3,10),(4,12);
starter: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT ___) AS unique_customers FROM orders;
solution: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers FROM orders;

title: Total Revenue
prompt: Compute total revenue as `revenue` = sum of `qty * price`.
schema:
CREATE TABLE sales(id INT, qty INT, price REAL);
INSERT INTO sales VALUES(1,2,10),(2,3,5),(3,1,20);
starter: SELECT SUM(___) AS revenue FROM sales;
solution: SELECT SUM(qty*price) AS revenue FROM sales;
hints: SELECT SUM(qty*price) AS revenue FROM sales

title: Headcount By Department
prompt: Return `department` and `headcount` (count of employees), ordered by department.
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, department TEXT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,'Eng'),(2,'Sales'),(3,'Eng'),(4,'HR'),(5,'Eng');
starter: SELECT department, ___ AS headcount FROM emp GROUP BY ___;
solution: SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS headcount FROM emp GROUP BY department ORDER BY department;

title: Avg Salary By Department
prompt: Return `department, avg_salary` per department, ordered by department.
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, department TEXT, salary INT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,'Eng',100),(2,'Eng',120),(3,'Sales',80),(4,'Sales',90);
starter: SELECT department, AVG(___) AS avg_salary FROM emp GROUP BY ___;
solution: SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary FROM emp GROUP BY department ORDER BY department;

title: Departments With 3+ Employees
prompt: Return `department` of teams with at least 3 employees.
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, department TEXT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,'Eng'),(2,'Eng'),(3,'Eng'),(4,'Sales'),(5,'HR'),(6,'HR');
starter: SELECT department FROM emp GROUP BY ___ HAVING ___;
solution: SELECT department FROM emp GROUP BY department HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3 ORDER BY department;

title: Revenue Per Product
prompt: For each `product_id`, return `revenue = SUM(qty*price)`. Order by revenue desc.
schema:
CREATE TABLE sales(product_id INT, qty INT, price REAL);
INSERT INTO sales VALUES(1,2,10),(2,1,50),(1,3,10),(2,2,50);
starter: SELECT product_id, SUM(___) AS revenue FROM sales GROUP BY ___ ORDER BY ___ DESC;
solution: SELECT product_id, SUM(qty*price) AS revenue FROM sales GROUP BY product_id ORDER BY revenue DESC;

title: High Earning Departments
prompt: Return `department` where average salary > 90.
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, department TEXT, salary INT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,'Eng',100),(2,'Eng',120),(3,'Sales',80),(4,'HR',95);
starter: SELECT department FROM emp GROUP BY ___ HAVING AVG(___) > ___;
solution: SELECT department FROM emp GROUP BY department HAVING AVG(salary) > 90 ORDER BY department;

title: Orders Per Month
prompt: Return `month` (YYYY-MM) and `order_count` per month, sorted by month.
starter: SELECT substr(created_at,1,7) AS month, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM orders GROUP BY ___ ORDER BY ___;
solution: SELECT substr(created_at,1,7) AS month, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM orders GROUP BY month ORDER BY month;

title: Order With Customer Name
prompt: Return `o.id, c.name, o.amount` joining orders to customers, ordered by o.id.
starter: SELECT o.id, c.name, o.amount FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON ___;
solution: SELECT o.id, c.name, o.amount FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id ORDER BY o.id;
hints: JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id

title: Customers Without Orders (LEFT JOIN)
prompt: Return `name` of customers with zero orders.
starter: SELECT c.name FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON ___ WHERE ___;
solution: SELECT c.name FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id WHERE o.id IS NULL ORDER BY c.name;

title: Total Spend Per Customer
prompt: Return `name, total_spend` for every customer (NULL→0), ordered by name.
starter: SELECT c.name, COALESCE(SUM(o.amount),0) AS total_spend FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON ___ GROUP BY ___;
solution: SELECT c.name, COALESCE(SUM(o.amount),0) AS total_spend FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id=c.id GROUP BY c.id, c.name ORDER BY c.name;

title: Employees And Their Managers
prompt: Return `employee_name, manager_name` for every employee. If no manager, show NULL.
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, name TEXT, manager_id INT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,'Alice',NULL),(2,'Bob',1),(3,'Carol',1),(4,'Dan',2);
starter: SELECT e.name AS employee_name, m.name AS manager_name FROM emp e LEFT JOIN emp m ON ___;
solution: SELECT e.name AS employee_name, m.name AS manager_name FROM emp e LEFT JOIN emp m ON m.id = e.manager_id ORDER BY e.id;
hints: Self-join: alias the same table twice.
LEFT JOIN emp m ON m.id = e.manager_id

title: Order Lines With Names
prompt: Return `customer_name, product_name, qty` joining orders→customers and orders→products. Sort by customer_name, product_name.
starter: SELECT c.name, p.name, o.qty FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON ___ JOIN products p ON ___;
solution: SELECT c.name AS customer_name, p.name AS product_name, o.qty FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id=o.customer_id JOIN products p ON p.id=o.product_id ORDER BY c.name, p.name;
hints: JOIN customers c ON ... JOIN products p ON ...

title: Products Never Sold
prompt: Return `name` of products with no rows in sales.
starter: SELECT p.name FROM products p LEFT JOIN sales s ON ___ WHERE ___;
solution: SELECT p.name FROM products p LEFT JOIN sales s ON s.product_id=p.id WHERE s.id IS NULL ORDER BY p.name;

title: All Users And All Orders
prompt:
List every (user_id, order_id) pair where either side exists. Missing side is NULL. Order by user_id NULLS LAST, then order_id NULLS LAST.

SQLite has no FULL OUTER — emulate with LEFT JOIN UNION.
solution:
SELECT u.id AS user_id, o.id AS order_id FROM users u LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.user_id=u.id
UNION
SELECT o.user_id, o.id FROM orders o LEFT JOIN users u ON u.id=o.user_id WHERE u.id IS NULL
ORDER BY user_id IS NULL, user_id, order_id IS NULL, order_id;
hints: ORDER BY x IS NULL, x sorts NULLs last in SQLite

title: Cartesian Pairing
prompt: Given `sizes(s)` and `colors(c)`, return every (s,c) pair as `size, color`, ordered by size then color.
starter: SELECT s.s AS size, c.c AS color FROM sizes s CROSS JOIN colors c;
solution: SELECT s.s AS size, c.c AS color FROM sizes s CROSS JOIN colors c ORDER BY s.s, c.c;

title: Customers Who Bought Product 10
prompt: Return distinct `name` of customers who ever bought product_id=10.
starter: SELECT DISTINCT c.name FROM customers c WHERE EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id=c.id AND o.product_id=10);
solution: SELECT DISTINCT c.name FROM customers c WHERE EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id=c.id AND o.product_id=10) ORDER BY c.name;

title: Above-Average Salary
prompt: Return `name` of employees earning above the overall average salary.
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, name TEXT, salary INT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,'A',50),(2,'B',100),(3,'C',150),(4,'D',80);
starter: SELECT name FROM emp WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(___) FROM emp);
solution: SELECT name FROM emp WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM emp) ORDER BY name;

title: CTE: High Spenders
prompt: Using a CTE, find customers with total order amount > 100. Return `customer_id, total`.
schema:
CREATE TABLE orders(id INT, customer_id INT, amount REAL);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES(1,1,50),(2,1,80),(3,2,40),(4,3,200);
starter:
WITH totals AS (SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id)
SELECT customer_id, total FROM totals WHERE ___;
solution:
WITH totals AS (SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id)
SELECT customer_id, total FROM totals WHERE total > 100 ORDER BY customer_id;

title: Highest Paid Per Department
prompt: Return `name, department, salary` of the highest-paid employee in each department.
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, name TEXT, department TEXT, salary INT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,'A','Eng',100),(2,'B','Eng',120),(3,'C','Sales',80),(4,'D','Sales',90);
starter: SELECT name, department, salary FROM emp e WHERE salary = (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM emp WHERE department = ___);
solution: SELECT name, department, salary FROM emp e WHERE salary = (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM emp WHERE department = e.department) ORDER BY department;
hints: = (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM emp WHERE department = e.department)

title: Users With No Orders (NOT IN)
prompt: Return `id, name` of users with no orders, using NOT IN.
starter: SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT ___ FROM orders);
solution: SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT user_id FROM orders WHERE user_id IS NOT NULL) ORDER BY id;

title: Two-Step CTE Pipeline
prompt: Step 1: per-customer totals. Step 2: rank them and return top 2 (customer_id, total).
schema:
CREATE TABLE orders(id INT, customer_id INT, amount REAL);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES(1,1,50),(2,2,200),(3,3,150),(4,1,80),(5,2,30);
starter:
WITH totals AS (SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id)
SELECT customer_id, total FROM totals ORDER BY total DESC LIMIT 2;
solution:
WITH totals AS (SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id)
SELECT customer_id, total FROM totals ORDER BY total DESC LIMIT 2;

title: Recursive: Numbers 1..5
prompt: Using a recursive CTE, return numbers 1 through 5 as column `n`.
schema: CREATE TABLE dummy(x INT); INSERT INTO dummy VALUES(0);
starter: WITH RECURSIVE seq(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM seq WHERE n<5) SELECT n FROM seq;
solution: WITH RECURSIVE seq(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM seq WHERE n<5) SELECT n FROM seq ORDER BY n;

title: ROW_NUMBER By Salary
prompt: Return `name, salary, rn` where rn is the row number ordered by salary DESC (overall, no partition).
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, name TEXT, department TEXT, salary INT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,'A','Eng',100),(2,'B','Eng',120),(3,'C','Eng',90),(4,'D','Sales',80),(5,'E','Sales',95);
starter: SELECT name, salary, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY ___) AS rn FROM emp;
solution: SELECT name, salary, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn FROM emp ORDER BY rn;

title: RANK vs DENSE_RANK
prompt: Return `salary, rk` where rk = DENSE_RANK over salary DESC. Use distinct salaries from emp.
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, salary INT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,100),(2,100),(3,90),(4,80);
starter: SELECT salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rk FROM emp;
solution: SELECT salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rk FROM emp ORDER BY salary DESC, rk;
hints: DENSE_RANK skips no values for ties.

title: Top Per Department
prompt: Return `name, department, salary` of the top earner in each department (use ROW_NUMBER partitioned by department).
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, name TEXT, department TEXT, salary INT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,'A','Eng',100),(2,'B','Eng',120),(3,'C','Eng',90),(4,'D','Sales',80),(5,'E','Sales',95);
starter:
WITH r AS (SELECT name, department, salary, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY ___ ORDER BY ___) AS rn FROM emp)
SELECT name, department, salary FROM r WHERE rn=1;
solution:
WITH r AS (SELECT name, department, salary, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn FROM emp)
SELECT name, department, salary FROM r WHERE rn=1 ORDER BY department;
hints: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC)

title: Previous Day Price (LAG)
prompt: For each `day`, return `day, price, prev_price` using LAG ordered by day.
schema:
CREATE TABLE prices(day INT, price REAL);
INSERT INTO prices VALUES(1,100),(2,105),(3,103),(4,110);
starter: SELECT day, price, LAG(price) OVER (ORDER BY day) AS prev_price FROM prices;
solution: SELECT day, price, LAG(price) OVER (ORDER BY day) AS prev_price FROM prices ORDER BY day;

title: Running Total
prompt: Return `day, amount, running_total` (cumulative sum ordered by day).
schema:
CREATE TABLE sales(day INT, amount REAL);
INSERT INTO sales VALUES(1,10),(2,20),(3,5),(4,15);
starter: SELECT day, amount, SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY ___ ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total FROM sales;
solution: SELECT day, amount, SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY day ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total FROM sales ORDER BY day;

title: Share Of Department Salary
prompt: Return `name, department, share` where share = ROUND(salary * 1.0 / SUM(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department), 2).
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, name TEXT, department TEXT, salary INT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,'A','Eng',100),(2,'B','Eng',120),(3,'C','Eng',90),(4,'D','Sales',80),(5,'E','Sales',95);
starter: SELECT name, department, ROUND(salary*1.0 / SUM(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY ___), 2) AS share FROM emp;
solution: SELECT name, department, ROUND(salary*1.0 / SUM(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department), 2) AS share FROM emp ORDER BY department, name;

title: Salary Quartiles (NTILE)
prompt: Return `name, salary, q` where q = NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY salary).
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, name TEXT, salary INT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,'A',60),(2,'B',70),(3,'C',80),(4,'D',90),(5,'E',100),(6,'F',110),(7,'G',120),(8,'H',130);
starter: SELECT name, salary, NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY ___) AS q FROM emp;
solution: SELECT name, salary, NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY salary) AS q FROM emp ORDER BY salary;
hints: NTILE(n) splits rows into n buckets.

title: First Login Per User
prompt: For each row, return `user_id, ts, first_ts` where first_ts is FIRST_VALUE(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts).
schema:
CREATE TABLE logins(user_id INT, ts INT);
INSERT INTO logins VALUES(1,100),(1,200),(2,150),(2,180),(1,300);
starter: SELECT user_id, ts, FIRST_VALUE(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY ___ ORDER BY ___) AS first_ts FROM logins;
solution: SELECT user_id, ts, FIRST_VALUE(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) AS first_ts FROM logins ORDER BY user_id, ts;
hints: FIRST_VALUE(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts)

title: Uppercase Names
prompt: Return `name` from users in UPPERCASE as `upper_name`, ordered by name.
schema: CREATE TABLE users(id INT, name TEXT); INSERT INTO users VALUES(1,'alice'),(2,'bob'),(3,'carol');
starter: SELECT UPPER(___) AS upper_name FROM users ORDER BY name;
solution: SELECT UPPER(name) AS upper_name FROM users ORDER BY name;

title: Full Name
prompt: Concatenate `first_name || ' ' || last_name` as `full_name`. Order by full_name.
schema: CREATE TABLE users(id INT, first_name TEXT, last_name TEXT); INSERT INTO users VALUES(1,'Ada','Lovelace'),(2,'Alan','Turing');
starter: SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name FROM users ORDER BY full_name;
solution: SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name FROM users ORDER BY full_name;

title: Short Usernames
prompt: Return `name` where LENGTH(name) <= 4. Order by name.
schema: CREATE TABLE u(id INT, name TEXT); INSERT INTO u VALUES(1,'Ann'),(2,'Bobby'),(3,'Cal'),(4,'Dwight');
starter: SELECT name FROM u WHERE LENGTH(___) <= 4 ORDER BY name;
solution: SELECT name FROM u WHERE LENGTH(name) <= 4 ORDER BY name;

title: Orders In 2024
prompt: Return `id` of orders where year of `created_at` (YYYY-MM-DD) is 2024.
schema:
CREATE TABLE orders(id INT, created_at TEXT);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES(1,'2023-12-01'),(2,'2024-01-15'),(3,'2024-07-20'),(4,'2025-01-01');
starter: SELECT id FROM orders WHERE substr(created_at,1,4) = '2024' ORDER BY id;
solution: SELECT id FROM orders WHERE substr(created_at,1,4) = '2024' ORDER BY id;

title: Days Between Events
prompt: Return `id, days` where days = julianday(end_date) - julianday(start_date) (integer).
schema:
CREATE TABLE events(id INT, start_date TEXT, end_date TEXT);
INSERT INTO events VALUES(1,'2024-01-01','2024-01-10'),(2,'2024-02-01','2024-02-05');
starter: SELECT id, CAST(julianday(end_date) - julianday(start_date) AS INT) AS days FROM events ORDER BY id;
solution: SELECT id, CAST(julianday(end_date) - julianday(start_date) AS INT) AS days FROM events ORDER BY id;

title: Salary Buckets
prompt: Return `name, bucket` where bucket = 'low' if salary<80, 'mid' if <120, else 'high'. Order by name.
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, name TEXT, salary INT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,'A',60),(2,'B',100),(3,'C',150),(4,'D',80);
starter: SELECT name, CASE WHEN salary < 80 THEN 'low' WHEN salary < 120 THEN 'mid' ELSE 'high' END AS bucket FROM emp;
solution: SELECT name, CASE WHEN salary < 80 THEN 'low' WHEN salary < 120 THEN 'mid' ELSE 'high' END AS bucket FROM emp ORDER BY name;
hints: CASE WHEN salary<80 THEN 'low' WHEN salary<120 THEN 'mid' ELSE 'high' END

title: Conditional Counting
prompt: Return `paid_count` and `cancelled_count` as a single row using SUM(CASE...).
schema:
CREATE TABLE orders(id INT, status TEXT);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES(1,'paid'),(2,'cancelled'),(3,'paid'),(4,'pending'),(5,'paid');
starter: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN status='paid' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS paid_count, SUM(CASE WHEN status='cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cancelled_count FROM orders;
solution: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN status='paid' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS paid_count, SUM(CASE WHEN status='cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cancelled_count FROM orders;
hints: SUM(CASE WHEN status='paid' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)

title: Email Or Fallback
prompt: Return `id, email_or_unknown` where missing emails show 'unknown'.
schema: CREATE TABLE u(id INT, email TEXT); INSERT INTO u VALUES(1,'a@x.com'),(2,NULL),(3,'c@x.com');
starter: SELECT id, COALESCE(email,'unknown') AS email_or_unknown FROM u ORDER BY id;
solution: SELECT id, COALESCE(email,'unknown') AS email_or_unknown FROM u ORDER BY id;
hints: COALESCE is cleaner than CASE here.
Same as CASE WHEN email IS NULL.

title: Pivot Status By Day
prompt: For each `day`, return `day, paid, cancelled` (counts) sorted by day.
schema:
CREATE TABLE orders(id INT, day INT, status TEXT);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES(1,1,'paid'),(2,1,'cancelled'),(3,2,'paid'),(4,2,'paid'),(5,3,'cancelled');
starter: SELECT day, SUM(CASE WHEN status='paid' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS paid, SUM(CASE WHEN status='cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cancelled FROM orders GROUP BY day ORDER BY day;
solution: SELECT day, SUM(CASE WHEN status='paid' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS paid, SUM(CASE WHEN status='cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cancelled FROM orders GROUP BY day ORDER BY day;
hints: SUM(CASE WHEN status='paid' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)

title: UNION Two Sources
prompt: Return distinct emails from both `web_users` and `app_users` sorted A→Z.
schema:
CREATE TABLE web_users(email TEXT); INSERT INTO web_users VALUES('a@x.com'),('b@x.com');
CREATE TABLE app_users(email TEXT); INSERT INTO app_users VALUES('b@x.com'),('c@x.com');
starter: SELECT email FROM web_users UNION SELECT email FROM app_users ORDER BY email;
solution: SELECT email FROM web_users UNION SELECT email FROM app_users ORDER BY email;

title: Web-Only Emails (EXCEPT)
prompt: Return emails in web_users but NOT in app_users, sorted.
schema:
CREATE TABLE web_users(email TEXT); INSERT INTO web_users VALUES('a@x.com'),('b@x.com'),('d@x.com');
CREATE TABLE app_users(email TEXT); INSERT INTO app_users VALUES('b@x.com'),('c@x.com');
starter: SELECT email FROM web_users EXCEPT SELECT email FROM app_users ORDER BY email;
solution: SELECT email FROM web_users EXCEPT SELECT email FROM app_users ORDER BY email;

title: Top 2 Salaries Per Department
prompt: Return `department, name, salary` for the top 2 earners in each department. Ties broken by name.
schema:
CREATE TABLE emp(id INT, name TEXT, department TEXT, salary INT);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES(1,'A','Eng',100),(2,'B','Eng',120),(3,'C','Eng',90),(4,'D','Sales',80),(5,'E','Sales',95);
starter: WITH r AS (SELECT department, name, salary, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC, name) AS rn FROM emp) SELECT department, name, salary FROM r WHERE rn<=2 ORDER BY department, rn;
solution: WITH r AS (SELECT department, name, salary, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC, name) AS rn FROM emp) SELECT department, name, salary FROM r WHERE rn<=2 ORDER BY department, rn;

title: Deduplicate Keep Latest
prompt: Given events with possible duplicates (same `user_id`), return one row per user — the one with the largest `ts`. Columns: user_id, ts, payload.
schema:
CREATE TABLE events(user_id INT, ts INT, payload TEXT);
INSERT INTO events VALUES(1,100,'a'),(1,200,'b'),(2,150,'c'),(2,180,'d'),(3,50,'e');
starter: WITH r AS (SELECT user_id, ts, payload, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts DESC) AS rn FROM events) SELECT user_id, ts, payload FROM r WHERE rn=1 ORDER BY user_id;
solution: WITH r AS (SELECT user_id, ts, payload, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts DESC) AS rn FROM events) SELECT user_id, ts, payload FROM r WHERE rn=1 ORDER BY user_id;

title: Funnel: View → Add → Buy
prompt: Given `events(user_id, step)` (step in 'view','add','buy'), return per-step user count: step, users. Sort step in funnel order view,add,buy.
schema:
CREATE TABLE events(user_id INT, step TEXT);
INSERT INTO events VALUES(1,'view'),(2,'view'),(3,'view'),(1,'add'),(2,'add'),(1,'buy');
starter: SELECT step, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS users FROM events GROUP BY step ORDER BY CASE step WHEN 'view' THEN 1 WHEN 'add' THEN 2 WHEN 'buy' THEN 3 END;
solution: SELECT step, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS users FROM events GROUP BY step ORDER BY CASE step WHEN 'view' THEN 1 WHEN 'add' THEN 2 WHEN 'buy' THEN 3 END;
hints: CASE in ORDER BY for funnel order

title: Consecutive Login Streak
prompt: For user 1, find the longest run of consecutive `day` values in logins. Return the streak length as `streak`.
schema:
CREATE TABLE logins(user_id INT, day INT);
INSERT INTO logins VALUES(1,1),(1,2),(1,3),(1,5),(1,6),(1,8);
starter: WITH g AS (SELECT day, day - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY day) AS grp FROM logins WHERE user_id=1) SELECT MAX(c) AS streak FROM (SELECT grp, COUNT(*) AS c FROM g GROUP BY grp);
solution: WITH g AS (SELECT day, day - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY day) AS grp FROM logins WHERE user_id=1) SELECT MAX(c) AS streak FROM (SELECT grp, COUNT(*) AS c FROM g GROUP BY grp);
hints: Trick: day - ROW_NUMBER() is constant in a run.

title: Sessionize Events (30-min gap)
prompt: Mark each event with a `session_id` (per user), incrementing when gap to prev event > 30. Return user_id, ts, session_id ordered by user_id, ts.
schema:
CREATE TABLE events(user_id INT, ts INT);
INSERT INTO events VALUES(1,0),(1,10),(1,50),(1,120),(2,0),(2,5);
starter: WITH g AS (SELECT user_id, ts, CASE WHEN ts - LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) > 30 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS new_sess FROM events) SELECT user_id, ts, SUM(new_sess) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) + 1 AS session_id FROM g ORDER BY user_id, ts;
solution: WITH g AS (SELECT user_id, ts, CASE WHEN ts - LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) > 30 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS new_sess FROM events) SELECT user_id, ts, SUM(new_sess) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) + 1 AS session_id FROM g ORDER BY user_id, ts;
hints: Cumulative SUM of flags = session id

title: Reverse A String
prompt:
Implement `reverse_string(s)` returning the string reversed.

Example: `reverse_string('abc') -> 'cba'`.
solution:
def reverse_string(s):
    return s[::-1]

title: Palindrome Check
prompt: Implement `is_palindrome(s)` (case-insensitive, ignore spaces).

title: Count Vowels
prompt: Implement `count_vowels(s)` returning the count of a,e,i,o,u (case-insensitive).
solution:
def count_vowels(s):
    return sum(1 for c in s.lower() if c in 'aeiou')

title: Anagram Check
prompt: Implement `is_anagram(a,b)` returning True if a and b are anagrams (ignore case & spaces).

title: Most Common Character
prompt: Implement `most_common_char(s)` returning the most frequent character. Break ties by first appearance.

title: Run-Length Encode
prompt: Implement `compress(s)` returning RLE like 'aaabbc' -> 'a3b2c1'.

title: Longest Word
prompt: Implement `longest_word(s)` returning the longest space-separated word. Ties: return the first.
solution:
def longest_word(s):
    return max(s.split(), key=len) if s.split() else ''

title: Sum Of Positives
prompt: Implement `sum_positive(nums)` summing only positive numbers.
solution:
def sum_positive(nums):
    return sum(n for n in nums if n>0)
hints: sum(n for n in nums if n>0)

title: Second Largest
prompt: Implement `second_largest(nums)` returning the 2nd-largest *distinct* number. Return None if not possible.

title: Flatten One Level
prompt: Implement `flatten1(xs)` flattening a list of lists by exactly one level.
solution:
def flatten1(xs):
    return [x for sub in xs for x in sub]
hints: [x for sub in xs for x in sub].

title: Unique Preserving Order
prompt: Implement `unique(xs)` removing duplicates while keeping first occurrence order.

title: Running Maximum
prompt: Implement `running_max(nums)` returning a list where element i = max of nums[0..i].

title: Chunk Into Groups
prompt: Implement `chunk(xs, n)` splitting xs into sublists of size n (last may be shorter).
solution:
def chunk(xs, n):
    return [xs[i:i+n] for i in range(0,len(xs),n)]

title: Matrix Sum
prompt: Implement `matrix_sum(m)` returning the total sum of all elements in 2D list m.
solution:
def matrix_sum(m):
    return sum(sum(row) for row in m)

title: Transpose Matrix
prompt: Implement `transpose(m)` returning the transpose of a non-empty rectangular 2D list.
solution:
def transpose(m):
    return [list(row) for row in zip(*m)]

title: Rotate 90° Clockwise
prompt: Implement `rotate90(m)` rotating a square matrix clockwise 90°.
solution:
def rotate90(m):
    return [list(row) for row in zip(*m[::-1])]

title: Max Per Row
prompt: Implement `row_max(m)` returning a list with the max of each row.
solution:
def row_max(m):
    return [max(r) for r in m]

title: Spiral Order Traversal
prompt: Implement `spiral(m)` returning elements of m in spiral order (top-row, right-col, bottom-row reversed, left-col upward).

title: Word Frequency
prompt: Implement `word_freq(s)` returning a dict mapping each lowercased word to its count. Words split on whitespace.

title: Group Words By Length
prompt: Implement `group_by_len(words)` returning a dict {length: sorted list of words}.

title: List Intersection
prompt: Implement `intersect(a,b)` returning sorted list of values present in both (deduplicated).
solution:
def intersect(a,b):
    return sorted(set(a) & set(b))

title: Two-Sum Indices
prompt: Implement `two_sum(nums, target)` returning a tuple (i,j) with i<j such that nums[i]+nums[j]==target, or None if none. Use O(n) dict.

title: First Non-Repeating Char
prompt: Implement `first_unique(s)` returning the first character that appears exactly once, or '' if none.

title: Group Anagrams
prompt: Implement `group_anagrams(words)` returning a list of groups; each group sorted; whole result sorted by group's first word.
solution:
def group_anagrams(words):
    from collections import defaultdict
    d=defaultdict(list)
    for w in words: d[''.join(sorted(w))].append(w)
    groups=[sorted(g) for g in d.values()]
    groups.sort(key=lambda g:g[0])
    return groups

title: Invert Dict
prompt: Implement `invert(d)` swapping keys and values. Assume values are unique.
solution:
def invert(d):
    return {v:k for k,v in d.items()}

title: Sort Strings By Length
prompt: Implement `sort_by_len(words)` returning words sorted by length ascending, ties alphabetical.
solution:
def sort_by_len(words):
    return sorted(words, key=lambda w:(len(w), w))

title: Top K Frequent
prompt: Implement `top_k(nums, k)` returning the k most frequent numbers, sorted by frequency desc then value asc.
solution:
def top_k(nums, k):
    from collections import Counter
    c=Counter(nums)
    return [n for n,_ in sorted(c.items(), key=lambda kv:(-kv[1], kv[0]))[:k]]

title: Dense Rank Scores
prompt: Implement `dense_rank(scores)` returning a list of dense ranks (1=highest). Same score → same rank, no gaps.

title: Sort Records
prompt: Implement `sort_records(rs)` sorting list of dicts by 'age' asc then 'name' asc; return list.
solution:
def sort_records(rs):
    return sorted(rs, key=lambda r:(r['age'], r['name']))

title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
prompt: Implement `merge(a,b)` merging two sorted lists into one sorted list in O(n+m) without using sorted().
solution:
def merge(a,b):
    i=j=0; out=[]
    while i<len(a) and j<len(b):
        if a[i]<=b[j]: out.append(a[i]); i+=1
        else: out.append(b[j]); j+=1
    out.extend(a[i:]); out.extend(b[j:])
    return out

title: FizzBuzz Up To N
prompt: Implement `fb(n)` returning list of strings 1..n: 'Fizz' on multiples of 3, 'Buzz' on 5, 'FizzBuzz' on both, else str(i).

title: Factorial
prompt: Implement `factorial(n)` for n>=0 iteratively.
solution:
def factorial(n):
    r=1
    for i in range(2,n+1): r*=i
    return r

title: Is Prime
prompt: Implement `is_prime(n)` returning True if n is a prime > 1.
solution:
def is_prime(n):
    if n<2: return False
    if n<4: return True
    if n%2==0: return False
    i=3
    while i*i<=n:
        if n%i==0: return False
        i+=2
    return True

title: Count Down By Step
prompt: Implement `countdown(start, step)` returning a list from start down to 0 (inclusive) decreasing by step. If would skip 0, stop above 0.
hints: Stop when next would go negative.

title: Fibonacci (Memoized)
prompt: Implement `fib(n)` with n>=0. Use recursion + memoization or iteration.
solution:
def fib(n):
    a,b=0,1
    for _ in range(n): a,b=b,a+b
    return a

title: Recursive Sum
prompt: Implement `rsum(xs)` recursively summing the list.

title: Deep Flatten
prompt: Implement `deep_flatten(xs)` flattening arbitrarily nested lists to a single flat list (preserving order).

title: Fast Power
prompt: Implement `power(x, n)` for integer n>=0 in O(log n) using recursion (exponentiation by squaring).

title: Balanced Parentheses
prompt: Implement `balanced(s)` using a stack: True if every (, [, { has a matching closer in the right order.

title: BFS Shortest Path Length
prompt: Given adjacency dict `g` and start, end, implement `shortest_len(g,start,end)` returning the shortest number of edges, or -1.

title: Reverse Linked List (As List)
prompt: Treat the input list as a singly-linked list (head→tail). Implement `reverse_ll(xs)` returning a new list reversed iteratively.

title: K Smallest Elements
prompt: Implement `k_smallest(nums,k)` returning the k smallest in ascending order using heapq.

title: Stack With O(1) Min
prompt:
Implement `apply(ops)` simulating a min-stack. Each op is one of:
- ['push', v]
- ['pop']
- ['min']
Return a list of results for every 'min' op in order. Assume no 'min' on empty stack.

title: Process Tasks By Priority
prompt: Implement `process(tasks)` where tasks is a list of (priority, name); lower priority = processed first. Return names in processing order. Stable for ties.
solution:
def process(tasks):
    import heapq
    h=[(p,i,n) for i,(p,n) in enumerate(tasks)]
    heapq.heapify(h)
    out=[]
    while h: out.append(heapq.heappop(h)[2])
    return out

title: Binary Search Index
prompt: Implement `bsearch(arr, target)` returning index of target in sorted arr, or -1.
solution:
def bsearch(arr, target):
    lo,hi=0,len(arr)-1
    while lo<=hi:
        mid=(lo+hi)//2
        if arr[mid]==target: return mid
        if arr[mid]<target: lo=mid+1
        else: hi=mid-1
    return -1

title: Pair Sum In Sorted
prompt: Implement `pair_sum(arr, target)` for SORTED arr. Return (i,j) indices i<j with arr[i]+arr[j]==target, or None. O(n) two-pointer.
hints: Move inward by sum vs target.

title: Max Average Subarray K
prompt: Implement `max_avg(arr,k)` returning the max average of any contiguous subarray of length k.

title: Count Connected Components
prompt: Given undirected graph as adjacency dict, implement `components(g)` returning the number of connected components. Nodes = keys of g.

title: Merge Intervals
prompt: Implement `merge_intervals(ivs)` merging overlapping [start,end] intervals; return sorted list.

title: Longest Substring Without Repeats
prompt: Implement `longest_unique(s)` returning length of the longest substring with all unique characters. O(n) sliding window.
solution:
def longest_unique(s):
    last={}; left=0; best=0
    for i,c in enumerate(s):
        if c in last and last[c]>=left: left=last[c]+1
        last[c]=i
        if i-left+1>best: best=i-left+1
    return best

title: O(n) Has Duplicate
prompt: Implement `has_duplicate(nums)` returning True if any value repeats. Must be O(n) average time.
solution:
def has_duplicate(nums):
    return len(set(nums)) != len(nums)

title: Count Pairs Summing To K
prompt: Implement `count_pairs(nums,k)` counting unordered pairs (i<j) with nums[i]+nums[j]==k. O(n) with dict.
solution:
def count_pairs(nums,k):
    from collections import Counter
    seen=Counter(); n=0
    for x in nums:
        n += seen[k-x]
        seen[x]+=1
    return n
hints: For each x, look up k-x in seen counter.

title: O(n) Anagram
prompt: Implement `anagram_n(a,b)` in O(n) without sorting (use a counter).
hints: O(n) vs sort O(n log n).

title: Refactor Nested Loop
prompt: Given two lists, implement `common(a,b)` returning True if any value of a appears in b. Must be O(n+m).

title: Parse Naive CSV
prompt: Implement `parse_csv(text)` where text is lines separated by '\n', fields by ','. First line is header. Return list of dicts mapping header→value. Empty text → [].
solution:
def parse_csv(text):
    if not text: return []
    lines=text.split('\n')
    head=lines[0].split(',')
    return [dict(zip(head, l.split(','))) for l in lines[1:] if l]

title: Dedupe Records By Key
prompt: Implement `dedupe(records, key)` keeping the FIRST record for each value of records[i][key]. Preserve input order.

title: Validate Records Against Schema
prompt: Implement `validate(records)` returning a list of booleans: each record must have integer 'a' and string 'b' (use isinstance).
solution:
def validate(records):
    return [isinstance(r.get('a'),int) and isinstance(r.get('b'),str) for r in records]

title: Aggregate Sales By Region
prompt: Given list of dicts {region, amount}, implement `total_by_region(rows)` returning dict {region: total amount}.

title: Clean Whitespace & Empties
prompt: Implement `clean(values)` taking list of strings — strip whitespace and drop empty/None values. Return cleaned list preserving order.

title: Pivot Long To Wide
prompt: Given rows like {date, metric, value}, implement `pivot(rows, metrics)` returning list of dicts: one per date with each metric as a column (missing→0). Sort by date asc.
